risk_of_bias.support_text: A biostatistician who was not involved in the clinical care of participants prepared sealed, consecutively numbered and opaque envelopes.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Not possible to blind participants and personnel to the treatment

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment (detection bias): Participant-assessed outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Participants were not blinded to treatment group

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment (detection bias): Personnel-assessed outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the outcome assessors (trained study physiotherapists) of other study outcomes and doctors carrying out the follow-up visits were blinded to group allocation when possible"

risk_of_bias.domain: Incomplete outcome data (attrition bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Losses were clearly reported and reasonably balanced between groups. However, loss for 12-month data was 24% overall.
Prospectively registered with clinical trials register (NCT01758835). Reported outcomes are consistent with planned outcomes
We identified no other sources of bias.

characteristics.methods:
RCT, parallel design, 3‐armed trial
Review comparison:removable ankle support versus non‐removable ankle support (after non‐surgical fracture management)
characteristics.participants:
Total number of randomised participants:247
Source population:multicentre; 2 major trauma centres in Finland
Inclusion criteria:all skeletally mature participants (≥ 16 years) with an isolated Weber B type fibular fracture and congruent ankle mortise (medial clear space < 4 mm and ≤ 1 mm wider than the superior clear space) in the primary radiographs visiting the emergency departments; ability to walk unaided before the current trauma and enrolment < 7 days after the injury
Exclusion criteria:previous deltoid ligament injury (a history of ankle twisting injury requiring cast treatment), other important fracture in the affected ankle or foot area, current bilateral ankle fracture, concomitant tibial fracture, pathological fracture, diabetic or other neuropathy, inadequate cooperation, or permanent residence outside the catchment area
Baseline characteristics for intervention group (3‐week orthosis)


Age, mean (SD):46 (± 19) years

Sex (m/f):36/44

Surgery:none had surgery

Fracture severity:not reported

Baseline characteristics for intervention group (3‐week cast)


Age, mean (SD):46 (± 17) years

Sex (m/f):43/40

Surgery:none had surgery

Fracture severity:not reported

Baseline characteristics for control group


Age, mean (SD):45 (± 18) years

Sex (m/f):46/38

Surgery:none had surgery

Fracture severity:not reported
characteristics.interventions:
Timing of randomisation:in the emergency department
Treatment management for both groups:participants received guidance from a physiotherapist on walking with crutches. Weight‐bearing was permitted in all 3 groups immediately after the application of the cast or orthosis. Participants received written and verbal instructions on how to cope with the ankle fracture and support (cast and orthosis groups) and how to remove and reapply the support (orthosis group)
Intervention group


Number randomised:80; overall losses: 14 (3 lost contact, 11 withdrew); number analysed at 12 weeks: 73; number analysed at 12 months: 66

Treatment management:orthosis (Dynacast/Ortho‐Glass; BSN medical, Rutherford College, NC) for 3 weeks. Participants in the 3‐week orthosis group were instructed to wear the brace as necessary or according to comfort. They could take off the orthosis when needed and move their ankle freely. At 3 weeks, the orthosis was removed. Participants were instructed to continue weight‐bearing as tolerated with no need for further bracing or support.

Intervention group


Number randomised:83; overall losses 10 (2 lost contact, 7 withdrew, 1 died); number analysed at 12 weeks: 75; number analysed at 12 months: 73

Treatment management:below‐the‐knee cast for 3 weeks. At 3 weeks, the cast was removed. Participants were instructed to continue weight‐bearing as tolerated with no need for further bracing or support.

Control group


Number randomised:84; overall losses 11 (4 lost contact, 7 withdrew); number analysed at 12 weeks: 73; number analysed at 12 months: 73

Treatment management:cast for 6 weeks
characteristics.outcomes:
Timing of assessments:6 and 12 weeks, and at 52 weeks (additional clinical follow‐up also at 3 weeks)
End of treatment:6 weeks
End of follow‐up:52 weeks
Included in this review:activity limitation, QoL, impairment measures of pain, adverse events.
Other outcomes measured in the study:fracture union, ankle dorsiflexion range of motion, plantarflexion range of motion
Note: in the primary analysis, we compared the 3‐week orthosis group with the 6‐week cast group
characteristics.notes:
Study dates:December 2012 to June 2016
Funding sources:supported by state funding for university level health research (Oulu University Hospital and Tampere University Hospital)
Declarations of interest:1 study author declared an honorarium for a lecture. Other study authors declare no support from any organisation for the submitted publication, no financial relationships with any organisation that might have an interest in the submitted work in the previous 3 years; no other relationships or activities that could appear to have influenced the submitted work

sections.text: In this paper by Kortekangas and colleagues (BMJ 2019;364:k5432, doi:10.1136/bmj.k5432, 23 January 2019), table 2 contained several errors. In the correct version (see linked file) the order of devices and duration of wear have been transposed in each of the headings for the last two columns and the term “cast” added to the final column for six week duration. The numbers and percentages for nerve compression and plaster/orthosis sore have been aligned correctly and the point estimate for three week orthosis versus six week cast has been rounded to 0.1.

review_title: Rehabilitation for ankle fractures in adults
